name: Merrow
description: Merrow, from Irish mythology, are mermaid-like creatures inhabiting the seas around Ireland. The name comes from the Irish 'muir-óigh,' meaning 'sea maiden.' They are often described as beautiful, alluring beings, both male and female, though female merrow are more commonly featured in tales. Unlike the stereotypical mermaid with a fish tail, female merrow typically possess a salmon-like tail or a seal skin cloak (cohuleen druith) that allows them to transform between human and sea creature forms. Male merrow are often described as uglier, with green teeth, red noses, and coarse hair. They are believed to be able to control the weather and summon storms. Merrow are sometimes benevolent, rescuing drowning sailors or even intermarrying with humans, but they can also be dangerous, luring people to watery graves or causing shipwrecks. Their connection to the sea makes them powerful figures, and their ability to shift between worlds highlights the liminal space between the human and natural realms in Irish folklore. Merrow represent the allure and danger of the ocean, embodying both beauty and unpredictable power. They are often associated with music and song, using their enchanting voices to lure sailors. The taking of their cohuleen druith by a human male is a common motif in folklore, forcing the merrow to remain on land as his wife.
subtitle: Sea Maidens and Mermen of the Deep
appearance: Female merrow are beautiful with long flowing hair, often green, and possess a salmon-like tail or a magical seal skin cloak (cohuleen druith). Male merrow are often described as ugly, with green teeth, red noses, and coarse hair. Both genders have webbed hands and feet.
habitat: The seas and oceans surrounding Ireland, particularly in deeper waters and underwater caves.
behavior: Merrow can be benevolent, rescuing drowning sailors, or malevolent, causing shipwrecks and luring people to their deaths. They are often associated with music and are said to have enchanting voices. Some merrow are said to intermarry with humans.
weaknesses: Loss of Cohuleen Druith (seal skin cloak)

keyMyths.title: The Cohuleen Druith
keyMyths.description: A human man steals a female merrow's seal skin cloak, forcing her to remain on land as his wife. She eventually finds the cloak and returns to the sea, often leaving children behind.

keyMyths.title: Merrow's Benevolence
keyMyths.description: Merrow are sometimes said to rescue drowning sailors or warn ships of impending storms. This highlights their complex relationship with humans.

keyMyths.title: Merrow's Curse
keyMyths.description: Angering a merrow can bring misfortune upon a village or individual, including storms, shipwrecks, and drowning. This showcases their power over the sea.

sources.author: Lady Wilde
